blocks.text: Chapter 6.
Adventures in Mental Time Travel
The ideas in 30 seconds

We chronically favor our present self over our future selves (temporal discounting); deliberately summoning past and future selves into a decision — via tools like Suzy Welch's 10/10/10 — counteracts the bias.
Emotional flooding ("tilt") shrinks our time horizon to the current moment; zooming out to the long view, as if reading a year-long stock chart instead of the live ticker, restores perspective.
Regret arrives after decisions, when it can no longer help; moving it in front of the decision converts a useless emotion into a planning tool.

Ulysses contracts — precommitments made when we are rational that bind or nudge our future, less-rational selves — either raise barriers to bad behavior or lower barriers to good behavior, and at minimum interrupt autopilot.
A decision swear jar is a personal list of bias-signaling words and thoughts (certainty claims, "I knew it," luck-blaming) that triggers a stop-and-vet moment.

Scenario planning, backcasting, and Gary Klein's premortem map the tree of possible futures in advance; prospective hindsight boosts reason-finding by about 30%, and a mapped future defuses hindsight bias after the outcome arrives.
Night Jerry Robs Morning Jerry: Why We Shortchange Our Future Selves

Jerry Seinfeld's stand-up bit captures the core problem: Night Jerry stays up late having fun, because getting up exhausted is Morning Jerry's problem. Morning Jerry hates Night Jerry but is powerless — he can't retaliate, except by oversleeping and hurting a still-later Jerry. Every one of us runs this con on ourselves.

The mechanism is temporal discounting: we weight immediate rewards far more heavily than future costs, even when the trade is objectively terrible. It's why retirement saving is so hard — the person who pays for tonight's splurge is a stranger decades away. The decisions that feel best right now are frequently the ones our future self will pay for.

Duke's fix is mental time travel: deliberately bringing your past and future selves into the room when you decide. When a choice is being made only by the present self, it's being made by the self with the worst incentives. Recruiting Past You (who has seen how these choices played out before) and Future You (who inherits the consequences) creates a more balanced negotiation — a kind of internal decision committee spanning time.

A ready-made tool is business writer Suzy Welch's 10/10/10: before deciding, ask "How will I feel about this in ten minutes? In ten months? In ten years?" The exact intervals matter less than the exercise — it forcibly drags future versions of you into a moment that would otherwise be ruled entirely by in-the-moment feeling. The device works retrospectively too: asking how you felt about similar past choices ten minutes, ten months, and ten years later recruits your past selves as witnesses.

None of this eliminates temporal discounting — it's wired in. But like every debiasing tool in the book, it shifts the odds: more decisions get made by the whole timeline of you rather than by tonight's Night Jerry.
The Ticker and the Tilt: Zooming Out When Emotions Zoom In

Imagine watching your happiness the way a nervous investor watches a stock ticker. Moment to moment, every downtick feels like a catastrophe — and because losses hurt roughly twice as much as equivalent gains feel good, a flat tire in the rain can wreck a day that is, on any longer chart, going fine. Zoom out to the one-year or ten-year view and the blip disappears into the trend. The blip doesn't deserve a panic sell; neither does most of what upsets us in the moment.

Poker players have a name for what happens when we fail to zoom out: tilt. After a string of losses or a brutal bad beat, emotions flood the system, the rational deliberative mind goes offline, and players start making wild, destructive decisions "from the emotional center." Tilt isn't just a poker phenomenon — it's any state where in-the-moment feeling hijacks decision quality: the blowout argument, the rage-quit, the revenge email.

The defenses are temporal. First, learn your own tilt signature — the physical and verbal warning signs (heat in the face, rising voice, repeating grievances) — so you can recognize the state before it makes your decisions for you. Second, precommit to not making decisions while tilted: step away from the table, sleep on the email. Third, apply mental time travel in the moment: "Will this matter in a year? How do I feel now about the version of this that happened a year ago?" Usually the honest answer deflates the crisis.

This is also where regret becomes useful. Kahneman has noted that regret is among our most painful emotions — but it arrives after the decision, when it can only punish, not protect. The trick is to move regret in front of the decision: preview it. Ask, before acting on tilt, "How am I going to feel tomorrow about what I'm about to do?" Anticipated regret, unlike retrospective regret, can actually change the choice. 10/10/10 is exactly this maneuver, systematized.
Ulysses Contracts and the Decision Swear Jar: Binding Your Future Self

Ulysses knew the Sirens' song would make him steer toward the rocks, so he had his crew plug their ears and tie him to the mast — a decision made by rational Present Ulysses that constrained irrational Future Ulysses. A Ulysses contract is any such precommitment, and it comes in two flavors.

Barrier-raising contracts make the bad choice harder or impossible: a poker player leaving her bankroll at home so she can't rebuy on tilt, a dieter keeping no junk food in the house, a spender cutting up credit cards, an alcoholic taking Antabuse. Barrier-lowering contracts make the good choice easier or automatic: automatic 401(k) payroll deductions, pre-chopped vegetables at eye level, scheduling the workout with a partner who'll be waiting.

Even weak contracts earn their keep, because every precommitment installs a decision-interrupt: a speed bump that forces at least a moment of reflection at exactly the point where autopilot would otherwise take over. You can still break the contract — but now you have to notice you're breaking it, and that notice is often enough to re-engage deliberation.

Duke's portable version is the decision swear jar. Just as a swear jar makes you catch and pay for forbidden words, you commit to a watch-list of words and thought patterns that signal motivated reasoning, and treat catching one as a trigger to stop and vet the belief. The list includes: expressions of overconfidence and certainty ("I'm 100% sure," "there's no way"); absolutes like "always," "never," "worst," "best"; signs of resulting or hindsight bias ("I knew it," "I told you so," "I should have known"); complaining about bad luck while banking good outcomes as skill; zero-sum blame and gloating over others' failures ("they're idiots"); signals you're performing to an audience rather than truthseeking; and shutting off information because it threatens your narrative. The jar doesn't fine you money — it charges you a moment of honest self-examination, which is worth more.
Scouting the Future: Scenario Planning and Backcasting

Poker players can't know which card comes next, so they get good at reconnaissance on the future: mapping the set of possible outcomes and estimating the probability of each before committing chips. That habit — scenario planning — transfers directly to life and business. Before a decision, list the realistic ways the future could unfold and assign each a rough likelihood, exactly the way an oddsmaker prices a race.

The payoffs are threefold. First, better choices: a decision is a bet on futures (Chapter 2), and you can't price a bet without enumerating the outcomes. Second, preparedness: futures you've imagined can't blindside you, and you can pre-position responses to the bad branches. Third, better group decisions and communication: when a team agrees in advance on the scenarios and their probabilities, later disagreements become disciplined arguments about estimates rather than after-the-fact blame.

Scenario planning gets more powerful when you work backward instead of forward. In backcasting, you time-travel to a successful future — imagine the headline "We hit the goal," written from a date down the road — and then reconstruct the story of how you got there. Working backward from the achieved outcome surfaces the necessary intermediate steps, the resources you'll need, the decision points along the way, and the low-probability breaks that would have to fall your way. If the success story requires three long-shot events in a row, better to learn that now, while the plan can still be changed or the goal resized.

Backcasting is cheerful by design — it interrogates the happy branch. That's exactly why it can't stand alone: a plan built only from the success story inherits our natural optimism. It needs a pessimistic twin.
The Premortem: Prospective Hindsight and the 30% Advantage

The twin is the premortem, championed by decision researcher Gary Klein. Where a postmortem dissects a failure that already happened, a premortem is held before the decision: the team time-travels to a future in which the project has failed, and each member's job is to write the story of why. Not "what could go wrong?" but "it went wrong — what happened?"

That tense shift is the active ingredient. Deborah Mitchell, J. Edward Russo, and Nancy Pennington found that prospective hindsight — imagining that an event has already occurred, rather than merely might occur — increased people's ability to correctly identify reasons for future outcomes by about 30%. Treating the failure as an accomplished fact unlocks explanations the "maybe" framing leaves hidden.

The premortem also does social work. In most organizations, voicing doubts reads as disloyalty; a premortem makes pessimism the assignment, so dissenters can surface risks without penalty — institutionalized devil's advocacy in miniature (Chapter 5). It rewards the person who finds the fatal flaw instead of punishing them. Paired with backcasting, it completes the map: the routes to success and the routes to failure, each with rough probabilities attached. Some imagined failures you can now prevent; others you can only prepare for; and some plans, seen whole, deserve to be abandoned — a cheap lesson at premortem prices.

There's one more dividend, paid after the outcome arrives. Hindsight bias works by collapse: once one future becomes fact, all the alternatives evaporate from memory and the result feels like it was inevitable — "we should have known." But if you mapped the tree of futures in advance, you have a record proving the actual outcome was just one branch among many, with an estimated probability attached. A 20% disaster that happens doesn't prove the decision was wrong; your own pre-decision map says so. Mapped futures thus protect decision quality twice — before the bet, by improving it, and after, by letting you field the outcome honestly (Chapter 3) instead of resulting on yourself.
In practice
Before your next consequential decision, run 10/10/10 in writing: how will you feel about each option in ten minutes, ten months, and ten years?
Write down your personal tilt signature — three physical or verbal warning signs — and precommit to a rule: no irreversible decisions (emails, trades, ultimatums) while any sign is present.

Set up one barrier-raising Ulysses contract (remove the tempting default: delete the app, unstock the snack, cap the account) and one barrier-lowering contract (automate the good default: auto-transfer to savings, pre-scheduled workout).

Start a decision swear jar: list five phrases you catch yourself using ("I'm sure," "I knew it," "so unlucky," "always/never," "they're idiots") and treat each catch as a cue to stop and vet the belief behind it.

For a current project, backcast from success — write the future headline and the steps that produced it — then hold a premortem: assume it failed, list every plausible cause in past tense, and adjust the plan for the preventable ones.
Save your scenario map (outcomes plus probabilities) before deciding, and reread it when the outcome lands, so hindsight bias can't rewrite what you actually knew.
